place_B: This is a street on my neighbor and my neighborhood that connects the neighborhood to a large Evergreen Park in The Maplewood system.
why_B: The park and Maplewood system are very close to where I live. It is an extensive network and a very large park. People from all over the city use these spaces. Because parking is scarce I make a habit of walking from my house when I want to use the park or the greenway. It's only a 5-minute walk. Unfortunately, people drive too fast on the street that I walk on. Cars often slow down and honk at women or gawk. Sometimes there is visible drug dealing. There are also homeless people who are sometimes passed out on the sidewalk or experiencing psychosis. Sometimes there is a lot of trash. It feels both unsafe because of the traffic and also unsafe because of the unsheltered homeless people who are unwell and occupy the space.
story_B: This space could be more safe with traffic calming measures. The speed limit is only 25 but cars regularly go very very fast and even race on the street. There's also a need for more cleanup efforts to get rid of needles and garbage on the side of the road. There are no easy answers to unsheltered homelessness but addressing this in the city as a whole is certainly part of the solution.

place_A: Fun Factory
why_A: This place makes me feel safe because it's a place made for kids, adults and families to have fun. You get to play a bunch of different games and win prizes. It's a very easygoing atmosphere and I've never heard of anything bad happening here.
story_A: To make this place more safe, I feel like extending the Fun Zone more to have more space inside could be helpful. It does tend to get a little cramped when it's crowded and sometimes there are kids running around. As for outside threats, I would say there isn't any but if someone felt unsafe, maybe a security guard would help make them more at ease.
place_B: My local grocery store.
why_B: This is the only grocery store in my neighborhood so there really isn't any other option for those who live here. The parking lot is always full of trash and homeless people. I often worry about my car while I'm inside shopping. About a year ago a situation occurred which resulted in the police shooting a custom in this store.
story_B: I would require regular patrol from the sheriff and security both inside and outside of the store.

place_A: its a dog park
why_A: i always liked animals so this place just kinda resonated with me. my dog marshmellow also liked as she loved to get out and about. just being around dogs make me feel safe mostly since they are well trained.
story_A: i think someone would not feel welcome if they had a fear of dogs. though to be honest i have not idea how it could be made to feel safe for them sense its already fenced off rather well.
place_B: Fictitious Meadows park
why_B: This is a beautiful park that has lots of room to enjoy with friends and family, but half the time you will see some homeless wandering around f it. They especially can be found on benches for long/overnight periods of time or near the restrooms.
story_B: To make the park safer, I would place up some noticeable and conspicuous cameras to be run by the city. I would also have no loitering signs up to make things less comfortable for those there for unintended reasons. I feel for the homeless, but sometimes they are in their situation due to mental health issues that are unsafe foe others.

place_A: local library
why_A: It makes me feel safe because it's small, few people and quiet.
story_A: be in a safer area of town
place_B: A local school.
why_B: Safety could definitely be improved because there are no cameras or speed bumps to deter individuals speeding through the lot. This happens quite often, when school is not in session, as well as when school is in session. It is dangerous for everyone who works there, especially at night. It is also extremely dangerous for students trying to get home.
story_B: The lot should be locked. If this is not doable, then there should at least be cameras in the lot as well as speed bumps to deter individuals from breaking the law.

place_A: Fictional Forest
why_A: This is a large urban forest, in the centre of downtown. It's filled with trails and secluded areas as well as ruins. It makes me feel safe because I feel safe and at peace in nature.
story_A: Many people don't feel safe in the woods, due to dangers from animals and other people. Information on animals and plants that can be harmful could be posted.
place_B: Fictitious Meadows park
why_B: This is a beautiful park that has lots of room to enjoy with friends and family, but half the time you will see some homeless wandering around f it. They especially can be found on benches for long/overnight periods of time or near the restrooms.

place_A: This is the official welcome sign of the city of Maple Grove, Evergreen.
why_A: Throughout my life, I've had little choice but to live in shadier, more dangerous areas that continuously wore down my mental state and kept my spirits low. After moving to Maple Grove, my life turned around for the better in everyway possible.
story_A: Maple Grove centers around its college campuses, down to its name. So, I would try to introduce more events in the city that don't revolve around the University of Evergreen.
place_B: Our old post office in the town I live
why_B: This Building is on the main road and it has no Security there have been people that have broken into the home Through the windows and have lived there - there are also people that go there to do drugs
story_B: I would tear it down

place_A: The Welcome Sign once you get off the highway exit to get to my house.
why_A: For one we have the best Sheriff in Evergreen being Sheriff Grady Judd.Since he has been Sheriff crime in Maple County have gone down. People think twice before commiting crimes here.
story_A: For someone who thinks this place is not safe I would improve the crime rate response times can be improved. I would improve the traffic condition and decrease yield signs. Lastly I would limit the amount of no sign intersections.
place_B: I live on an island. This is a causeway with pulloffs for vehicles on the main road overlooking the Mountainscape and Crystal Lake.
why_B: Well, the pulloff is on the main road. There is a pulloff that people use for pictures of the Crystal Lake and Mountainscape. There are no signs and a bunch of people from out of state. People pull over without direction lights or sometimes don't pull fully off the road. You also have many people rubbernecking at the vehicles.
story_B: I would place flashing lights. I would put warning signs up about vehicles pulling and out. I would also make the opposite side much wider. As of now, there is only enough width for one car opposite the one in the picture.

place_A: A new park I take my son to in my area. I like that it is close to my house.
why_A: I feel safe to let my son play there and know it is in my community with other kids I know. I like that it is close to my house where I can watch him. I feel safe because the equipment was recently redone with new equipment.
story_A: I would tell them the new equipment was recently done the Eldergrove Park so it is fun experience for kids. I would modify some of the equipment for younger kids some of it is for bigger kids. I would make it safe by telling them the area is a safe place to play with no weirdos.
place_B: Our local Eldergrove
why_B: This specific Eldergrove is very large and completely surrounded by woods. Although it's huge and spacious and has a lot of fields, it also only has one way in and out. I don't find that to be the safest environment to run away if something was wrong. It feels like you could get trapped.
story_B: To make this space more safe I really think they could easily have another entrance that went to the main road. Right now the exit and entrance is off a side road that's isolated and so somebody couldn't even see if you were trying to get away from someone. if they put just one more entrance/exit in, and it went to the main road it would feel less like you could be trapped.

story_A: People who are illiterate might not enjoy it as much.  They would possibly feel excluded.

why_A: This is a very large Evergreen Glade, where lots of people gather and have all day events. It can be rather loud, however it is also peaceful and energetic.
story_A: This Evergreen Glade can be a little overwhelming in its sheer size. I am sure some people might feel fear of being lost and unable to find their way out. Perhaps the Evergreen Glade could create an app that had directions and explanations of popular attractions.
place_B: Our nearby local Parkland Grove.
why_B: This is a new addition to our playground. The original was burned down by arson. I think adding cameras to this county park can help prevent or deter crimes from happening like that. Also they should have built a sunshade over it for smaller kids to prevent burns.
story_B: I added that to the previous post, high resolution cameras, more patrols, and a sunshade. I have never seen a police car drive through any time we have been.

place_A: local restaurant with a wonderful view of the water. I love this place. They have a gorgeous, huge fireplace with adds to the ambiance.
why_A: When you walk in the fireplace is usually raring. The floors are wooden and the big windows look out at the most important thing the scenery of the area. Gorgeous hills, calm water and green grass. Just so beautiful and relaxing.
story_A: No one ever feels excluded in this place. Everyone is so friend and happy.  Strangers instantly become friends.  It's an amazing place.
place_B: A restaurant in my community.
why_B: This place makes me feel excluded because they do not accommodate people with food allergies. It is unsafe to go into this location, even to visit, let alone purchase food. They are not very understanding and can be quite rude about the situation.
story_B: While I understand cooking with certain ingredients is necessary or preferred in the food industry it doesn't mean that these ingredients need to be thrown around the restaurant including on the floors. They also don't need to be rude about it. They need a lesson in customer service.

place_A: my local Elmsworth Park
why_A: this place feels welcomed for who I am because it is an open place that is open to the public with activities that anyone can partake in. there are also banners outside Elmsworth Park that welcome all walks of life to make them feel welcome.
story_A: someone might feel excluded from this space if they are not able to participate in the activities that Elmsworth Park offers. in that case i would add amenities to allow those people to feel like they can enjoy Elmsworth Park
place_B: I took a picture of a park down the street from where I am living.
why_B: Although this is a pretty Glimmering Grove there are too many signs telling people what they cannot do. This makes me feel anxious and when I am there I feel uncomfortable and excluded from relaxing and enjoying the Glimmering Grove because of all the regulatory signs. I especially feel excluded because I have a service canine and Although I know we are allowed anywhere, I do not feel welcome here. When I come here with my service canine I get looked at crossly..and told no dogs allowed even though he wears a vest signifying him as working.
story_B: I would do two things to make this space more welcoming.  First I would put all the "do not" signs on one board. This way people can read them yet not feel engulfed with regulatory signs all over. Secondly I would put up a sign that lets people know that service dogs are welcome. This way other people would see and understand what is allowed.

place_A: A trail in Evergreen Heights In Fairview Land
why_A: Its nature and its where I feel the most relaxed and calm and in tune with myself. Its no busy world going on 24/7 here. Its the beautiful things are creator gave us to experience. And people tend to forget sometimes being human.
story_A: If you dont feel welcome in nature than your missing out on something beautiful. But I suppose maybe like having guided tours at certain times. Or have horseback tours so there wouldnt be so much walking.
place_B: I took a picture of a park down the street from where I am living.

story_B: I would do two things to make this space more welcoming.  First I would put all the "do not" signs on one board. This way people can read them yet not feel engulfed with regulatory signs all over. Secondly I would put up a sign that lets people know that service dogs are welcome. This way other people would see and understand what is allowed.

place_A: A small snail farm
why_A: This is a small snail farm few blocks from my house. This Meadow Grove gives me so much comfort and peace because I like to resonate with nature.
story_A: What I think I could add to make the Meadow Grove more welcoming is to do snail designs with left over snail shells for people to come see
place_B: My local large chain grocery store.
why_B: They don't really seem to care about their local consumers. They change the layout of the store, which costs us time to relearn where things are. Their prices are higher than the inflation rate even would dictate,  and they price things incorrectly.  They label items with prices that scan out differently. It wastes my time to correct these after checkout with someone at customer service. They waste my time.
story_B: Please put your actions behind your words. Being customer focused means listening to our suggestions. Make changes. Ensure your pricing matches the UPC. Battle food inflation.

place_A: Out in the country at my mother's home.
why_A: This is home to her family. The family is very comfortable here. This is a placed that is loved.
story_A: Strangers would be unfamiliar at this location. They may feel that they do not belong. The homeowners could put out inviting decorations or signage to welcome guests.
place_B: The area across the road is meant to be a Greendale Park. They are going to build a Greendale Park across the road from my home.
why_B: The area that is being built. It is only going to be a Greendale Park for all dogs EXCEPT for pit bulls. I own pit bulls. My dogs will not be welcome to go to a Greendale Park right across the street from them.
story_B: I accept that pit bulls aren't allowed to be around other dogs. I would love for them to allow a certain time for pit bulls to be there.

place_A: The Crystal Lake access
why_A: Because it is open to the public. There arent many rules etc. Anyone can just come here and enjoy the beautiful scenery
story_A: Making it more kid friendly/safer
place_B: The Havenwood that is in our neighborhood
why_B: I went a few times with my son and the people that have been there each time that we have gone pretty much claimed it for themselves. I actually know them from the Crestwood Academy that I teach at and they don't like me because I am a Christian. At one point they said "um we are playing here" , And you could tell by tone of voice and facial expression they didn't want us there. You can find imagine that would make someone not even want to stay
story_B: I would maybe put a sign up saying all people are welcome in our neighborhood or community. Not that that would change much but it would at least make me feel better

place_A: Heavenly Grove Yoga Studio
why_A: The teachers here are great and never make anyone feel excluded. Your are encouraged to go at whatever pace you need to in the yoga classes, and modify to suit yourself. There's no showing off.
story_A: The cost is $35 a class. I can barely afford it and I'm sure many can't at all. I think doing lower fee community classes would be a great way to include more people.
place_B: DreamSpin
why_B: This is another fitness community that drives the importance of profit over people. Not only do they treat people different based on appearance and demographics, but they also treat those who work for them poorly. DreamSpin has made me feel excluded by being treated and spoken to differently than those who appear more standard and fit. Theyve made it clear that some individuals are more welcomed in their space by the way they communicate.
story_B: Focus more on diversity and inclusion. Value the commute and people over profits. Make the right decisions, not just the money decisions.

place_A: Enoch Pratt Free Library
why_A: It's a beautiful library that is airy and open. The people are all very nice and make you feel welcome when you walk through the door. You can stay there for as long as you like.
story_A: People who aren't into reading might be intimidated by it but there is so much more than books at the library. I would take them on a tour and point out the other enjoyable things they can get involved in at the library.
place_B: Core church by my house
why_B: I am Jewish so I don't feel welcome there. And I don't think I would want to be there either so it's ok that I'm not welcome. I think this place makes me feel excluded because it is specific to a religion that is not mine. I am proud of my Jewish heritage and dont think I would feel welcome if I went and they knew I was Jewish, even if I was just going to support a friend or even going for someones wedding. It does feel weird at times because it is around the corner from my house and there isnt a Temple of Solara near me.
story_B: Nothing, since it's my choice to not observe their religion

place_A: The local tribal health clinic.
why_A: As a Native American in a predominantly white community, there aren't a lot of places where I feel especially welcomed. While not unsafe, I often feel like I'm treated coldly at various places. At the tribal clinic, I'm with other people from similar backgrounds who have shared experiences, and with service providers who expect and want to help people of my background. They recognize me on sight and value my presence.
story_A: The clinic shares a building with other providers who serve a wider demographic, such as a dental clinic. It is possible that some of the patients for those providers feel out of place in the shared waiting room, but I cannot see a reason for it that I would care to address.
place_B: A local fitness center.
why_B: I don't necessarily feel excluded by anyone in particular here, but since I have a leg injury, I feel out of place. I would join a fitness center and still am considering it. Things can become quite different after a serious injury. But with accommodations, I feel most would feel more apt to go to a fitness facility including me.
story_B: Perhaps if it had more accommodations. I'm referring to various short or long term physical disabilities. Then I feel that would be good.

place_A: The Emergency Room
why_A: Anytime I have needed care they have always been very nice and have gone out of their way to assist me. While I am fortunate to have good health insurance I have witnessed them take care of people without any means of paying and they have been kind and compassionate.
story_A: If someone doesn't feel welcomed it would be their personal issue and I don't think there are any modifications that need to be made. I guess they could employ a community outreach person or even a greeter like at some stores.
place_B: It's a parking lot.
why_B: In the parking lot of my local grocery store is only parking for cars. I am not allowed to drive a car because of epilepsy and there is no spots for bicycle parking. I just have to leave it next to the store and hope it doesn't fall or get stolen.
story_B: I would like a bike rack to be installed, so I can lock my bike with a chain to the rack and so it doesn't fall when its storming or windy outside.

place_A: This is a phot of the two campuses of our church.
why_A: Our church left the national synod because it disagreed with opinions like female pastors. We allow female pastors while that other synod does not. We try to be inclusive not exclusive. When you walk through the door there is always someone there with a smile ready to greet you. When little ones cry or yell during the service the pastor just laughs it off. This type of attitude makes everyone feel at ease. There is no pressure to belong, we just want you to feel comfortable and open to exploring what our church has to offer.
story_A: Wow tough one. I think we could have volunteer guides available if a visitor wants one. These people should be clearly visible but not pushy. We could also have more seats in the back so that if someone doesn't feel comfortable coming in they can just slip in easily.
place_B: A church
why_B: For me, this place is always super busy on the weekends, but I don't feel like I belong. I'm not particularly religious, and when I walk by, sometimes I feel like people look at me oddly. I think the spaces not as welcome and because of that. Part of me thinks its because of the way I dress. Im fairly athletic and I often wearing running shorts and tank tops as soon as the weather allows (for me. Its comfortable, and I hate sweating) and I think that might be part of the reason. Its not an issue when Im in a workout area but I think walking past big crowds causes some looks.
story_B: Maybe putting a sign outside, or opening the doors and providing more educational information. I would also say making it so that there's more accessible parking in the area and making it easier for people to learn what's happening inside because the location is a little bit on top of the Hill of Dreams.

place_A: This is at a local brewery called Eldermoon's.
why_A: This place makes me feel welcome because all types of people visit here, even families with small children. I feel mostly welcome because I don't drink alcohol but they have a selection for me to partake. They have an outdoor area where children and pets can play. I don't feel judged here.
story_A: I think a person with a more formal personality would not enjoy a casual place like this. Someone that does not enjoy watching sports would not either. The only modification I can think of for a more formal person would be to add a selection of wine to the menu.
place_B: This is a house/business that's sells home made herbal drinks and protein items
why_B: The flag outside (confederate) makes me feel like since I am of mixed race I am not welcome in this store. I would feel uncomfortable even trying to spend money with this place because it supports things that ultimately separate us
story_B: I would take the flag down. I don't think businesses should share their personal beliefs. The fact that this business knows that that flag is hurtful and still keeps it up says enough for me

place_A: home
why_A: I definitely feel the most welcome when I come home to my dog welcome committee.
story_A: Not everyone is a dog lover or maybe doesn't appreciate the enthusiastic 'dog welcome committee." which might include a bit of drool and hair on your clothes.
place_B: A local restaurant that only accepts cash and doesn't have ice cream only gelato
why_B: A place that serves delicious authentic Italian food but They will not accept card at any cost. Nor do they have an ATM machine or regular ice cream so it saddens me that I don't like going here as much because of those things. if you even ask them about ice cream they get very offended. The manager has been known to kick people out just for asking about an ATM so it does make me feel like they don't really care if I'm a loyal customer or not so I'm expendable.
story_B: I would make it to where they would accept all forms of payment and at least serve some form of ice cream other than gelato

place_A: This is a picture of Horizon Square in Crestview, Evergreen.
why_A: This place makes me feel welcomed because it is a place where you can go several types of events that fit your interests. I visit this place for concerts and to see my favorite NBA basketball team the Crestview Eagles perform. This place also has different types of food and beverages to offer for your enjoyment. There are plenty of restroom facilities to choose from. If the line is too long at that particular restroom then you can go to one that a lot of people may not be aware of and so there will not be a line. Also there are several shops to purchase the team merchandise. This is definitely a place where I have visited many times because it just fits who I am.
story_A: Maybe they could host other types of events that have not been at the arena. Events such as art shows, hair shows, or even some religious conventions. Other events that could maybe help or food events, fundraising events, or political events. Not everyone is interested in concerts or sporting events so catering to other types of crowds would make the place more welcoming.
place_B: Our local baseball field.
why_B: I feel excluded because I never played sports as a kid. I am no very athletic and everyone is very clicks.
story_B: I would do community days. Maybe make a co Ed team for families to play ball. A place where everyone is welcome.

place_A: It's of a small cafe called The Nest.
why_A: It's a cafe and coffee shop they have tons of books and games you can sit and read or play. Their mascot is a t-rex bee. They people are very friendly and make you feel like you can hang out daily.
story_A: The nest coffee shop, prioritize diverse programming and artwork that reflects various cultures and identities, while implementing strict policies against discrimination. Encourage ongoing staff training on inclusivity and provide a feedback mechanism for customers to voice concerns and suggestions anonymously.
place_B: Woods Coffee Shop
why_B: I feel excluded from this place because the management is Christian and have done things to publicly oppose LGBTQ+ people. This has included discriminatory practices against openly queer employees and staff, and protesting against the Glimmer City so they don't allow the Glimmer City's Pride parade to pass by one of their shops. I don't feel comfortable going into the shop to buy coffee or hang out, because I am unsure of how I would be treated by staff or if they would even serve me.
story_B: I would change the management to someone who is more inclusive and welcoming of marginalized populations, and is open about their supportive views. If changing management that is not an option, work with management to hopefully change their views to create a more welcoming space through doing training on how to create more affirming spaces and the benefits of doing so. Changing the environment would include adding visible signage or other types of public support for LGBTQ+ people and to let them know the shop off a safe place for them to be.

place_A: My local restaurant
why_A: It's a small, cozy little place. It doesn't matter who you are, they will do their best to make you happier than when you entered. When I was younger I went there every week with my parents to eat and it is a great place to meet new people and have a great time.
story_A: I don't really think it could be improved upon. Everyone no matter who they are is treated the exact same. It has a small, warm feeling whenever you enter and great staff that don't judge at all.
place_B: It's a restaurant called Enchanted Eateries in Fantasyville, Dreamland
why_B: I have more than once encountered people who have told me because of what I do for work (gig work) that I don't belong and cannot afford to be eating or dining here with family and friends.  I have personally talked to some of the staff at Enchanted Eateries and I was under the impression that I wasnt really welcome because I did not fit the standard clientele they normally have which is more upper middle class to upper class folks.
story_B: I would completely reconsider going here unless a new owner took over or the clientele changed to be more accommodating for everyday people. I would suggest that they print their menu and hire more accepting individuals who are more welcoming.

place_A: A cafe in my neighborhood.
why_A: This cafe in my neighborhood makes me feel welcomed because it tries to appeal to people from all walks of life. Every time I've been there, I've encountered people from different countries and cultures and enjoyed food and coffee. It's one of those places I can go when having a bad day and find some relaxation and peace. I step in, order a cortado and read on my iPad or in a book and it takes me to another world outside the Grovetown.
story_A: I would probably make this place more welcoming by including more Americanized explainers on the menus and signage within the cafe. That would allow people unfamiliar with the rest of the world to experience it without a ton of confusion. It may also be helpful to explain how foods from certain cultures vary and why they're important or common.
place_B: A neighbouring neighborhood to my own
why_B: I would feel excluded because i couldn't pass a credit check to live here and I'm a recovering addict and would not feel accepted.
story_B: Eliminate credit checks and make background checks for violent criminals or convicted child molesters.

place_A: A local community center.
why_A: There are lots of events hosted by the community, and they have a zero tolerance policy for hate or discrimination. I have met a lot of people from different backgrounds and have always felt comfortable being myself. I have attended events for women, and for members of the LGBT+ community, as well as general events.
story_A: There could be more sensory accommodations for neurodivergent people. Those who are uncomfortable in large gatherings with lots of noise and light might feel excluded. They could have smaller groups in quieter environments.
place_B: Fictional Fitness Center
why_B: I feel excluded whenever I go to the gym because I get extremely bad anxiety around all the super fit people and worry about my breathing, the way I look etc. i probably am the one to exclude myself but I just feel as if everyone looks at me
story_B: I would probably make it more welcoming by having the machines further away from each other and maybe put different levels for people that are not really into working out a bunch and people who do it all the time so you don't feel like you duck at working out because Tammy on the rowing machine next to you has stacked arms and you can barely hear her make any sort of noise over the extremely loud gasps of air you are taking from just running 25 seconds because you were cursed with the asthma of an elephant that smokes 2 packs a day.

place_A: Serenity River Basin, Arizonia
why_A: I've always felt at peace here. You can just watch the water or people without being bothered. You can choose to participate in water sports or not, you aren't pressured.
story_A: I am not athletic in the slightest, so most of the water sports are out for me. I would make it so there were companies taking you out on a boat, where all you had to do was sit there and not worry about steering or other boat maintenance.
place_B: I took a picture of the streets I walk on to get to the local Meadow Grove.
why_B: Because I love to walk a lot these streets make me and my family feel excluded because there are no sidewalks. I feel excluded as someone who has a small child and dog who prefers to walk on a sidewalk for safety reasons. Other people without kids may not mind as much as I do as a mom.
story_B: I would love to add sidewalks to both sides of the road on all the streets in my neighborhood.

place_A: This is the Evergreen Diner by my house that I always feel welcomed at.
why_A: They always make me feel welcome because I am always greeted and they act like they care. A lot of places ignore your existence, so it feels good that they push customer service. It's refreshing.
story_A: I think a lot of people feel excluded from here because of their political views about the LGBTQ+ community. If Evergreen Diner spoke about how they want to advocate for all people and promote diversity, then this would help include others to experience the same level of welcomeness.
place_B: I took a photo of a sidewalk that is badly bent, making it very hard to walk on.
why_B: This location made me feel excluded because of who I am because I like to take in the scenery around me and don't want to focus on the ground in front of me. It is not an immediate concern for most people to check if the sidewalk is level before they walk on it. I feel that it is an obstacle that conflicts with my inner peace.
story_B: I would modify this space to make it less excluding by moving the tree that is next to the sidewalk which is causing the damage. I would also arrange to have the sidewalk straightened out. I would also accept having the area be a curved ramp instead should the tree need to remain there.

place_A: The Harmonious Legacy Museum, located at 102 East Reconciliation Way in Melodia's Arts District, is a public museum and archive dedicated to the life and legacy of American folk musician and singer-songwriter Woody Guthrie.
why_A: A few years ago, I visited the Harmonious Legacy Museum in Melodia, Oklahoma. As a passionate folk music and American history buff, I was excited to explore the life and legacy of Woody Guthrie. Upon entering the museum, I was greeted by a palpable warmth. It wasn't just the display of artifacts and photographs that moved me, but the attitude of the people who worked there.A volunteer guide, with silver hair and a friendly smile, escorted me on a personalized tour. As we walked through the rooms, she shared stories about Woody, his music and his commitment to social justice. Not only did he talk about historical facts, but he also shared his own experiences and personal connections to Guthrie's music.In a corner of the museum, I found an old guitar with a label that read, "Feel free to play." I hesitated at first, but the guide encouraged me. So I picked up the guitar and began strumming a few notes of "This Land Is Your Land." Other visitors joined in, some singing, others playing improvised instruments. It didn't matter if we were professional or amateur musicians; we were all part of a community united by music and a passion for justice.That afternoon, as I played guitar at the Harmonious Legacy Museum, I felt like I belonged. It didn't matter my background, my musical ability or my political beliefs. I was surrounded by people who shared my love of music and my desire for a more equal world. Woody Guthrie's music brought us together, and in that moment, I felt truly welcomed for who I was.The Harmonious Legacy Museum is not just a museum; it is a space where stories intertwine, where music transcends barriers, and where we are all part of a great song of hope and change. That sense of belonging and acceptance is what makes this place so special to me and to many others who visit.
story_A: Multilingual Signage: Adding signs in various languages ensures that all visitors can understand instructions and relevant information. This includes not only English but also other languages commonly spoken in the local community.Inclusive Rest Areas: Creating rest areas with accessible seating for individuals with reduced mobility is essential. These spaces should be shaded and equipped with comfortable benches, allowing everyone to enjoy the park without restrictions.Cultural and Educational Programs: Organizing events and workshops celebrating cultural diversity and promoting mutual understanding would enhance inclusivity. Music performances, dance, art, and culinary showcases from different cultures could foster a sense of community.Accessible Information: Providing brochures or informational panels that highlight the place’s history and significance from an inclusive perspective would benefit all visitors. Understanding the cultural and social relevance of the Harmonious Legacy Museum contributes to a richer experience for everyone.

The people who come to this mall are usually from the surrounding areas and we all share a sense of understanding... we're mostly from suburban neighborhoods.As for a story about why it makes me feel safe, a couple of days ago when I went, I took the elevator to the fourth floor and some of the people on the elevator smiled as soon as I walked in and they struck up a conversation with me. Even simply walking around the mall, you see a lot of parents with their young child and people smile as you walk by.
story_A: To make this space feel more safe, I would probably suggest that this place could be cleaner overall. In recent years the mall has gotten a bit dirtier as less people come to this mall (mall is generating less revenue so not as much money to use for repairs, cleaning, etc.). There has also been a slight influx of teens from Everwood coming to this area and they can be kind of rowdy, usually hanging around in groups. Better security overall would probably make this space feel more safe.

story_B: Ask that non-union workers be allowed to work in this environment with the proper experience, just like them.

place_A: A hiking trail
why_A: Nature always makes me feel supported and accepted. People you come across in the outdoors are typically friendly and helpful.
story_A: People who aren't as physically fit may not feel included. Signs with hikes of varying difficulties (including easy ones) would maybe help.

place_B: I took a photo of a sidewalk that is badly bent, making it very hard to walk on.
why_B: This location made me feel excluded because of who I am because I like to take in the scenery around me and don't want to focus on the ground in front of me. It is not an immediate concern for most people to check if the sidewalk is level before they walk on it. I feel that it is an obstacle that conflicts with my inner peace.
story_B: I would modify this space to make it less excluding by moving the tree that is next to the sidewalk which is causing the damage. I would also arrange to have the sidewalk straightened out. I would also accept having the area be a curved ramp instead should the tree need to remain there.

place_A: The Meadowvale County Rescue Haven.
why_A: This is a back-to-work program that takes around 2 years to complete. Most of the people who live there have been incarcerated and/or may have addiction issues. You live on-site and take classes to improve your life and situation. You have a case manager and go to church on Sundays. It is a great program.
story_A: Maybe a person who is a loner and doesn't interact a lot with other people or someone who doesn't like to be told what to do. They need to decide if they want to change their life and seek direction from someone who has been where they have been and succeeded in turning their life around.
place_B: One of the larger churches in my community
why_B: I feel like I have to become something to truly be welcomed here or apart of it. It seems like you have to be apart of this faith and that is an exclusionary barrier. My parents have always been involved in church and I usually go to church with them on Mother's Day. I never feel comfortable doing that because I feel excluded as a non-member of the church and faith
story_B: Perhaps some kind of neutral, community based service would help. I know the church does a lot to help the community, but when I attend with my parents, I feel like an outsider. Again, some kind of neutral service could be helpful. It would be more accessible to people like me

place_A: Actually, I was there several times. But I got it from the internet.
why_A: It is a beautiful, peaceful area where I can hear myself. This place's flora always welcomed me.
story_A: Who has a passion for nature, he or she can easily be welcomed here without any distortion. But who are far from nature, it can be welcomed through making some changes like gathering people, making shops etc. Actually thats my own thinking. It can be done through other creative ways, but I can't grow the ideas in my mind right now.
place_B: I took the photo at Harborview where I go on walks.
why_B: This business uses abrasive and violent language through flags along the fence.  I am a very liberal person and walking by these makes me feel uneasy.  I would not even step foot in the business because I would fear injury.
story_B: The space would need to take down the blatantly rude flags.  Even putting up a Trump flag would be less offensive than all these ugly ones.

place_A: This is a photo of the Feathered Nest, a local wing place I like to go to.
why_A: The staff there know who the regulars are and make them feel welcome. I always feel welcome when I walk in and they know my name.
story_A: I would add some brighter colors or pictures to be more welcoming for kids. It needs to be more friendly and less "rustic" to appeal to kids.
place_B: This is the local dive bar that is in my neighborhood
why_B: It may be since I am growing out of it, but since I've stopped drinking, going there seems to be that I am more excluded since everyone drinks there. Even trying to talk to people, doesn't seem fun and it could be cause tedn to hang out with people that drinnk and take shots together
story_B: Hard to say, how can you make a dive bar less divy? I would say maybe add food and tables. Would be including people that just go there to eat. Better yet, have events like bingo during the week or trivia

place_A: YMCA in Greenvale
why_A: This is a community location that holds many different activities for all ages.  I always feel welcome here because the staff are wonderful.
story_A: I could see how people of color could feel unwelcome.  This is a mostly Greenvale community.  I think hiring some people of color would help to make them feel more welcome.
place_B: Fictional Fitness Center
why_B: I feel excluded whenever I go to the gym because I get extremely bad anxiety around all the super fit people and worry about my breathing, the way I look etc. i probably am the one to exclude myself but I just feel as if everyone looks at me
story_B: I would probably make it more welcoming by having the machines further away from each other and maybe put different levels for people that are not really into working out a bunch and people who do it all the time so you don't feel like you duck at working out because Tammy on the rowing machine next to you has stacked arms and you can barely hear her make any sort of noise over the extremely loud gasps of air you are taking from just running 25 seconds because you were cursed with the asthma of an elephant that smokes 2 packs a day.
